---
title: "A primer to region-based methylation analyses"
subtitle: " "
excerpt: " "
date: 2026-05-12
author: "Konstanze Tan"
draft: false
# layout options: single, single-sidebar
layout: single
categories:
- long-read sequencing
---

Most existing methylation-based epigenome-wide association studies (EWAS) test one CpG at a time. While intuitive, this is at odds with how methylation biology actually works. Regulatory elements like promoters, enhancers and CpG islands span tens to hundreds of base pairs, and nearby CpGs tend to vary together across individuals. A genuine phenotype-linked shift spread across several CpGs can fly under the radar entirely if no single site clears the significance threshold.

This is the problem that region-based methylation analysis seeks to solve. Region-based methylation analysis gathers multiple votes and shifts the question from “is this CpG significant?” to “is this stretch of the genome consistently shifted?” In doing so, it can substantially increase the power to detect coordinated, biologically meaningful changes.

Several method families have been developed to tackle this regionalisation problem, each identifying differentially methylated regions (DMR) with a different philosophy. This post maps the core principles underlying four main method families, using a simple six-CpG toy locus to show how each approach sees the same data differently. The aim is a working intuition for what these methods are actually doing, so that choosing between them becomes a reasoned decision. It closes by how richer data from newer long-read sequencing technologies, which profile more CpGs with greater spatial continuity, make DMR the natural unit of association analysis, with some crucial adaptations.

# **What is a differentially methylated region?**

A differentially methylated region, or DMR, is a genomic interval containing multiple CpGs that show coordinated evidence of differential methylation.

While the common idea is that a DMR should capture a local, coordinated methylation signal rather than an isolated site, there are different ways to classify a DMR. One way is to look at the shape of the methylation signal along the genome. One could also find clusters by looking at the association p-values of neighbouring CpGs. These different starting points lead to subtly different definitions of what counts as a DMR.

# **Four families of DMR methods**

Existing methods for identifying DMRs can be organised into four families.

![image.png](image.png)

[](data:image/png;base64,)

Consider a toy locus with six CpGs:

| **CpG** | **Position** | **Case methylation** | **Control methylation** | **Difference** | **CpG p-value** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| CpG1 | chr1:10,050 | 50% | 49% | +1% | 0.80 |
| CpG2 | chr1:10,150 | 72% | 53% | +19% | 0.030 |
| CpG3 | chr1:10,210 | 75% | 55% | +20% | 0.020 |
| CpG4 | chr1:10,270 | 73% | 54% | +19% | 0.040 |
| CpG5 | chr1:10,880 | 54% | 53% | +1% | 0.70 |
| CpG6 | chr1:10,940 | 52% | 51% | +1% | 0.85 |

We will now look at how each of the four method families define a DMR within this toy locus.

## **Signal-smoothing methods**

Signal-smoothing methods look for local stretches where the methylation difference or association statistic is consistently shifted. The smoothing process reinforces consistent signals, while noisy and isolated signals are dampened.

![image.png](image-1.png)

[](data:image/png;base64,)

This family is useful when the expected biology is locally coherent, as is typical in promoters, enhancers, CpG islands, CpG shores, or other regulatory domains.

The main caution is that smoothing choices matter. A too-wide window can merge distinct signals; a too-narrow window can miss weak but consistent regional effects.

## **P-value aggregation**

P-value aggregation methods start with CpG-level association P-values. This is useful when you have already run an EWAS model like: methylation at CpG ~ phenotype + age + sex + covariates.

The main implementation of p-value aggregation is the *comb-p* tool. It first estimates how similar nearby CpG association signals tend to be as a function of genomic distance. It then uses this distance-dependent autocorrelation when combining neighbouring CpG-level p-values, so that clustered weak signals can accumulate regional evidence without being treated as fully independent. In effect, a region is strengthened by having nearby small p-values, but penalised for the correlation expected between CpGs at those distances.

![image.png](image-2.png)

[](data:image/png;base64,)

This family is useful when you already have a well-specified CpG-level association model. The autocorrelation step is what distinguishes it from naive Fisher’s method: p-values near each other are not independent, so the combination uses an empirical correlation structure.

The main limitation is that p-value clustering does not guarantee effect-size coherence. After getting a significant regional p, you still need to inspect whether all CpGs shift in the same direction and whether the effect is biologically meaningful.

## **Count-model methods**

Count-model methods are designed for sequencing-based methylation data. They can be thought of as EWAS models that test methylated reads out of total informative reads, so that higher-coverage methylation estimates naturally contribute more precise evidence than low-coverage estimates.

This is useful because the same methylation percentage can be supported by very different amounts of data. For example, at a single CpG, 75 methylated/100 total reads and 3 methylated/4 total reads both correspond to 75% methylation, but the first estimate is more reliable because it is supported by many more reads.

![image.png](image-3.png)

[](data:image/png;base64,)

Tools such as *DSS, RadMeth* and *dmrseq* use this count-aware logic to test for differential methylation. They model methylated out of total reads, often using beta-binomial or related frameworks that can account for biological variability across samples. In effect, a methylation difference is strengthened when it is supported by many reads across samples, and weakened when the same apparent difference is based on sparse or noisy coverage. The count-aware estimates are then used to call DMRs.

This family is useful for WGBS, RRBS, targeted bisulfite sequencing and long-read methylation data that can be summarised as methylated and unmethylated calls. However, for long-read methylation data, count models are not fully read-aware. Existing tools can account for coverage and often overdispersion across samples, but may not directly model methylation-caller uncertainty, molecule-level methylation patterns, haplotypes or read-level dependence.

## **Region-first methods**

Region-first methods flip the analytical order. Rather than first identifying CpGs associated with phenotype and then grouping them into regions, these methods first group CpGs into methylation units based on genomic features, then test those units against the phenotype.

The main implementation is *coMethDMR*. Starting from predefined regions, such as clusters of nearby CpGs within promoters, CpG islands, or gene bodies, *coMethDMR* identifies contiguous subsets of CpGs within each region whose methylation levels are correlated across samples. These co-methylated blocks are then tested for association with the phenotype.

![image.png](image-4.png)

[](data:image/png;base64,)

A key advantage of this design is that blocks are defined by the data’s correlation structure, without using the outcome variable. This reduces the risk of inflating the downstream association test by defining the tested regions from the same phenotype signal being evaluated.

The main limitation is that results depend on how input regions are selected and on the parameters used to define co-methylated blocks.

# **Association testing using long-read methylation data**

The advent of long-read sequencing technologies, which profile more CpGs with greater spatial continuity, makes the DMR a natural unit of association analysis. However, some important adaptations are required to the DMR methods described above. Long-read methylation data contain new layers of information that many existing DMR tools do not fully harness, including molecule-level methylation patterns, haplotype context uneven coverage, and methylation-caller uncertainty. By working only with methylation averages, existing methods can collapse distinct molecular states into the same summary value; for example, a region that is 50% methylated could reflect uniformly half-methylated molecules, or two haplotypes where one is fully methylated and the other is unmethylated. By neglecting read-depth or call-level confidence, methods can also overstate evidence from sparsely covered or uncertain sites.

A long-read DMR analysis should not only ask whether a region differs on average, but also whether that difference is well-supported by coverage, consistent across molecules, and where possible, interpretable in haplotype-aware terms.

Current long-read methylation association analysis are heterogeneous. Published work ranges from single site-level CpG association testing alongside DMR calling (Linga et al., 2025, *Front. Epigenet. Epigenom.*); to focused analyses of predefined episignature or known imprinted-loci (Geysens et al. 2025, *Genome Medicine*); to de novo segment-based testing (Rausch et al. 2023, *Cell Genom*).

One of the clearest long-read-specific frameworks for methylation association testing is  *pycoMeth*. *pycoMeth* builds on the MetH5 format, which stores methylation calls at read and base resolution, together with call uncertainty and read-group labels such as sample or haplotype. This means methylation does not have to be reduced immediately to CpG-level averages; the method can retain information about whether individual reads are confidently methylated, confidently unmethylated, or ambiguous, and whether those reads belong to particular samples or haplotypes. Using this granular evidence, pycoMeth defines coherent methylation segments with Bayesian changepoint detection, with read depth and call uncertainty influencing where boundaries are called. Once segments are defined, *pycoMeth* tests whether these regions are differentially methylated between samples, conditions or haplotypes.
